votes.reason: The Python code implements the exact same logic as the Coq code, summing the ASCII values of uppercase characters in the string.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq logic, summing the ASCII values of all uppercase characters in the string.
Both implementations sum the ASCII values of uppercase characters in the string.

The Python code faithfully implements the same logic as the Coq code: it iterates over characters, checks if each is an uppercase letter (ASCII 65-90), and sums their ASCII values. The spec check is identical.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_upper checks if a character's ASCII value is between 65 and 90 (uppercase letters), digitSum_calc sums the ASCII values of all uppercase characters in the string, and digitSum_spec checks if the result equals this sum. The iterative Python implementation produces the same results as the recursive Coq implementation for all concrete string inputs.

The Python code correctly implements the same logic as the Coq code: is_upper checks if a character's ASCII value is between 65 and 90 (uppercase letters), digitSum_calc sums the ASCII values of all uppercase characters in the string, and digitSum_spec checks if the result equals this sum. The iterative Python loop produces the same result as the recursive Coq function for all concrete string inputs.
Both sum ord(c) for characters with codes 65..90 (A-Z) and ignore others; the spec checks equality with that sum. Behavior matches on all ASCII inputs.
Both implementations detect ASCII uppercase characters (codes 65–90) and sum their code points; the spec checks equality with this sum, matching the Python behavior on all strings.
Both sum the ASCII codes (65–90) of uppercase letters in the string and the spec checks equality with that sum; behavior matches on all concrete inputs.
